skill.name: quality-reviewer
skill.description: Runs a structured code review that checks correctness, architecture, project standards, dependency versions, security issues, and current documentation.
skill.summary: Review code changes against project standards, current docs, dependency versions, and security concerns.
skill.author: ArcadeAI

security_audit.summary: Static external-command and weak-cryptography alerts are mostly false positives from Markdown fences, inline code formatting, and words such as description. The skill is still medium risk because it grants wildcard tool access, asks the agent to inspect project files, and requires web research.

medium_findings.title: Wildcard Tool Permission
medium_findings.description: The skill declares allowed-tools as '*' on line 4. This is not malicious by itself, but it gives the agent broad execution, filesystem, and network capability for a community skill.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The wildcard permission is explicit in the skill metadata. The risk depends on runtime safeguards and user trust, so it is medium rather than high.

medium_findings.title: Project Inspection and Web Research Workflow
medium_findings.description: The workflow asks the agent to list project context files and search or fetch current documentation. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it can expose local project structure or send dependency information to external services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file inspection and web research instructions are clear. They support the skill goal, but users should understand the privacy and egress implications.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported Ruby or shell backtick execution, but the cited locations are Markdown fences, inline file names, and output examples. No evidence found of executable Ruby backticks or command substitution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited text is visible Markdown formatting rather than executable code. There is no Ruby file or shell interpolation context in the skill.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported weak cryptography on lines 3 and 77. These lines contain descriptive text, not cryptographic algorithms, hash functions, ciphers, or security-sensitive operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines do not contain crypto code or instructions to use weak crypto. This appears to be a keyword collision with ordinary prose.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported system reconnaissance on line 119. That line asks whether library recommendations changed recently and does not request host, network, account, or system discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is about library documentation changes. No system reconnaissance behavior is present at that location.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Tool Access
dangerous_patterns.description: Wildcard tool access expands the impact of any prompt injection or mistaken instruction during review. Restricting tools to read, search, and browser operations would reduce risk.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The dangerous pattern is directly present in the skill metadata. The finding is contextual because broad access can be safe in trusted environments.

content.value_statement: Code reviews can miss outdated dependencies, changed documentation, and project-specific rules. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through a deeper review with current ecosystem checks.
actual_capabilities: Reviews recent code changes for correctness, edge cases, and error handling.
Checks local project standards such as CLAUDE.md, SAFEWORD.md, and architecture notes.
Evaluates whether new dependencies and abstractions are justified.
Verifies library versions and known security concerns through web research.
Compares implementation patterns against current official documentation.
Produces a concise verdict with critical issues and suggested improvements.
limitations: Requires network access to verify current versions and documentation.
Does not replace human review for business logic or product requirements.
May need repository context to provide accurate file and line feedback.
Broad tool access should be restricted in sensitive repositories.

use_cases.title: Pre-merge Pull Request Review
use_cases.description: Review changed files before merging and identify blocking correctness, security, or standards issues.

use_cases.title: Dependency Upgrade Check
use_cases.description: Confirm whether a library version is current and whether the implementation follows present documentation.

use_cases.title: Architecture Consistency Review
use_cases.description: Compare a proposed implementation with local architecture notes and established project patterns.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Change Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my recent changes for correctness, edge cases, and clear blocking issues. Keep the verdict concise.

prompt_templates.title: Standards Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these changes against the project standards files and architecture notes. Identify any rule violations or pattern mismatches.

prompt_templates.title: Dependency and Documentation Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this implementation against the latest official documentation and current stable versions for the libraries it uses.

prompt_templates.title: Full Release Readiness Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Perform a full quality review covering correctness, anti-bloat, elegance, standards, dependency versions, security issues, and current documentation.

output_examples.input: Review a pull request that adds a new React form and validation library.
output_examples.output: The review flags one blocking validation bug, notes that the dependency is current, and recommends using the project's existing form pattern.

output_examples.input: Check whether a Next.js implementation follows current documentation.
output_examples.output: The review confirms the stable version, identifies one deprecated API pattern, and provides a direct migration recommendation.

output_examples.input: Evaluate a small backend change before release.
output_examples.output: The review approves the logic, notes missing timeout handling, and separates the timeout issue from optional naming improvements.

best_practices: Use the skill after changes are available and the relevant files can be inspected.
Ask for latest documentation checks when dependency behavior or versions matter.
Treat critical findings as blockers and separate them from style preferences.
anti_patterns: Do not use the skill as a substitute for tests or production monitoring.
Do not ask it to browse external docs when repository policy forbids network access.
Do not accept broad refactor suggestions without checking release scope and ownership.

faq.question: What does this skill review?
faq.answer: It reviews correctness, edge cases, error handling, dependency choices, project standards, current versions, and official documentation.

faq.question: Does it require web access?
faq.answer: Web access is needed for its main value: checking current documentation, stable versions, and security advisories.

faq.question: Can it replace a human reviewer?
faq.answer: No. It supports reviewers by finding technical risks, but humans still own product intent and final approval.

faq.question: Can it review private repositories?
faq.answer: Yes, if the assistant is allowed to read the repository. Sensitive projects should restrict tool permissions and external browsing.

faq.question: What output should I expect?
faq.answer: Expect a concise quality review with category assessments, a verdict, critical issues, and suggested improvements.

faq.question: How is it different from a quick lint check?
faq.answer: It evaluates implementation intent, architecture fit, dependency freshness, and documentation alignment, not only formatting or syntax.
